did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically advanced Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures (InceptionV3, DenseNet121, ResNet50, and VGG16), for multi-class skin cancer classification on dermoscopic images from the HAM10000+ISC dataset. It also employed Explainable AI (XAI) methods like Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++ to interpret model predictions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of deep learning models (InceptionV3, DenseNet121, ResNet50, VGG16) for multi-class skin cancer classification using the HAM10000+ISC dataset. It includes specific methodology sections (dataset collection, data augmentation, model training), reports original quantitative results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score), and introduces Explainable AI (XAI) methods to interpret the models' predictions, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Explainable Deep Learning for Automated Skin Cancer Detection Using Advanced CNN Architectures on Dermoscopic Images
journal: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning solution for multi-class skin cancer classification using advanced Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures, specifically InceptionV3, DenseNet121, ResNet50, and VGG16, on the HAM10000+ISC dermoscopic image dataset. To address class imbalance, the authors employed data augmentation and class weighting strategies during model training and evaluation. The primary evaluation approach involved comparing the performance of these pre-trained models using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and ROC AUC. Among the tested architectures, InceptionV3 achieved the highest accuracy of 76% and an ROC score of 0.967. To enhance interpretability, the study integrated Explainable AI methods, including Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, and class-wise attention maps, to visualize the specific lesion areas influencing model predictions. The results demonstrate that the proposed system offers high classification accuracy and provides visual explanations that can assist dermatologists in early and accurate skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000+ISC dataset used for multi-class classification. The paper notes that attention maps for mel classes have elongated attention regions in the central part of lesions.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000+ISC dataset. The paper notes that attention maps for nv classes have elongated attention regions in the central part of lesions.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000+ISC dataset. The paper notes that attention maps for bcc classes are more focused, indicating predictions use localized features.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000+ISC dataset. The paper notes that attention maps for akiec classes are more focused, indicating predictions use localized features.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin cancer diagnosis (seven classes: mel, nv, bcc, akiec, vasc, df, bkl)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000+ISC dataset. The models (InceptionV3, DenseNet121, ResNet50, VGG16) predict discrete diagnostic categories. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC AUC. InceptionV3 achieved 76% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset downloaded from the Kaggle portal, containing images of seven classes of skin lesions. The study notes that the dataset has significant class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training the CNN models (InceptionV3, DenseNet121, ResNet50, VGG16) with class weighting and data augmentation to handle imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (loss plots and convergence analysis reported).
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation. The study reports specific counts for InceptionV3 testing: 2,003 total test samples, with 1,527 correctly classified and 476 misclassified.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address class imbalance in the HAM10000+ISC dataset by assigning higher importance to minority classes during model training.

statistical_methods_used.method: ROC AUC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the discriminative capability of the CNN models (InceptionV3, DenseNet121, ResNet50, VGG16) by analyzing the true positive rate against the false positive rate.
To visualize and quantify the number of correctly classified and misclassified samples for each model on the test set.
To monitor training and validation loss over epochs to assess model convergence and generalization.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC AUC) and visual comparisons (confusion matrices, loss curves, attention maps).

HAM10000+ISC.notes: InceptionV3 achieved the highest accuracy (76%) and ROC AUC (0.967). DenseNet121 was the second-best performer. VGG16 performed the worst across all metrics. The study utilized class weighting and data augmentation to handle class imbalance. Explainable AI methods (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++) were used to visualize attention maps, showing elongated attention regions for mel/nv classes and focused regions for bcc/akiec classes.

architecture_used.input_size: InceptionV3: 299x299; DenseNet121: 224x224; VGG16: 224x224; ResNet50: 128x128 (dataset resized to 128x128 for general processing)

architecture_details.InceptionV3: Multi-branch architecture with parallel convolutional filters and factorized convolutions.
architecture_details.DenseNet121: 121 layers with dense connections where each layer is connected to previous ones; includes Dense Blocks and transition layers.
architecture_details.ResNet50: 50-layer residual network with skip connections to facilitate information flow and prevent vanishing gradients.
architecture_details.VGG16: 16-layer convolutional neural network with small 3x3 filters and maxpooling blocks.

regularization: ReduceLROnPlateau (reduce factor 0.3 if no improvement in validation loss for 3 epochs)
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Class weighting using balanced class weight function from scikit-learn

classification_or_output_head.feature_extraction: Global Average Pooling (GAP) layer converts spatial feature maps into compact feature vectors.
classification_or_output_head.dense_layers: Dense Layer with 512-256 units and ReLU activation
classification_or_output_head.output: Multi-class classification (7 classes: nv, mel, bkl, bcc, akiec, vasc, df)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: hybrid models using CNNs and Transformers
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation using ImageDataGenerator in TensorFlow/Keras including: rotation (±25 degrees), horizontal/vertical shifting (±15%), zooming (±25%), shear transformation (0.1), and horizontal/vertical flipping
Class weighting using balanced class weight function from scikit-learn to handle class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated up to ±25 degrees.
Images were shifted horizontally or vertically up to ±15%.
A shear transformation of 0.1 was applied.
Images were flipped both horizontally and vertically.
Used to address class imbalance by assigning higher importance to minority classes during training, computed using the balanced class weight function from scikit-learn.

conclusions: InceptionV3 achieved the highest classification accuracy (76%) and ROC AUC (0.967) among the tested CNN architectures (InceptionV3, DenseNet121, ResNet50, VGG16) for multi-class skin cancer detection on the HAM10000+ISC dataset.
DenseNet121 was the second-best performing model with an accuracy of 74% and ROC AUC of 0.962, attributed to its dense connections facilitating feature reuse and gradient flow.

ResNet50 achieved moderate performance with 71% accuracy and an ROC AUC of 0.956, while VGG16 performed the poorest with 67% accuracy and an ROC AUC of 0.938, likely due to its uniform architecture's inability to effectively capture complex hierarchical features.

Explainable AI methods (Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++) successfully visualized model decision-making, revealing that models for melanoma (mel) and nevus (nv) classes focused on elongated central regions of lesions, whereas basal cell carcinoma (bcc) and actinic keratosis (akiec) predictions relied on more localized features.
The integration of XAI enhances trust and interpretability for dermatologists, addressing the 'black box' nature of deep learning models and supporting early and accurate diagnosis.

The study highlights that while deep learning shows promise, high accuracy for general skin lesion types remains challenging, and there is a need for systematic comparisons of CNN models to determine the best approaches for clinical adoption.
Class imbalance in the HAM10000+ISC dataset was addressed using class weighting and data augmentation, which are critical for preventing model bias towards majority classes.
important_remarks: The study is limited by its reliance on a single dataset (HAM10000+ISC), which may restrict generalizability to other populations, imaging devices, or clinical settings.
The approach relies solely on dermoscopic images and excludes patient-specific clinical metadata (e.g., age, gender, lesion site), which could improve diagnostic performance.
The models were not tested in a real-world clinical environment; validation was confined to a controlled experimental setting.
Recent architectures such as transformer models or hybrid convolutional-transformer models were not included in the comparison.
Class imbalance in the HAM10000+ISC dataset was addressed via class weighting and data augmentation, but the inherent imbalance remains a challenge for model bias.
High accuracy for general skin lesion types remains difficult to achieve, highlighting the need for systematic comparisons of CNN models for clinical adoption.
The study did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests; evaluation relied on descriptive metrics and visual comparisons.
